text.title: systemd User Services: Unit File Structure
text.summary: Unit/Service/Install sections, service types, restart policies, dependency types, and resource limit directives for systemd user services on Ubuntu 24.04.

rule.statement: Place application unit files in `~/.config/systemd/user/` and control them with `systemctl --user`. Never run application code as root via system services.
rule.rationale: User services are scoped to one user account, need no `sudo` to manage, and are isolated from system services. They work with the deploy script (rsync + venv) pattern and produce logs in `journalctl --user -u name`.

example.code: Location: ~/.config/systemd/user/nero-core.service Control: systemctl --user start|stop|restart|status nero-core Logs: journalctl --user -u nero-core -f Enable: systemctl --user enable nero-core (+ loginctl enable-linger)

section.title: Unit File Three-Section Structure

rule.statement: Every unit file has `[Unit]` (metadata + dependencies), `[Service]` (execution + limits), and `[Install]` (boot integration). `ExecStart` must use an absolute path.
rule.rationale: systemd resolves `ExecStart` before starting the service and rejects relative paths. A relative path silently fails to start the service with an uninformative error. Always use the full path from the venv: `/srv/nero/nero-core/.venv/bin/celery`.

example.code:
[Unit] Description=NERO Core (Celery Workers) After=network-online.target Wants=network-online.target StartLimitBurst=5 StartLimitIntervalSec=60
[Service] Type=simple WorkingDirectory=/srv/nero/nero-core/src EnvironmentFile=/home/nero/workspace/.env ExecStart=/srv/nero/nero-core/.venv/bin/celery -A nero_core.celery worker --loglevel=info Restart=on-failure RestartSec=5
MemoryMax=8G MemoryHigh=6G CPUQuota=400% LimitNOFILE=65536 OOMScoreAdjust=0
[Install] WantedBy=default.target

section.note: Put `StartLimitBurst` in `[Unit]`, NOT in `[Service]`. In `[Service]` it is silently ignored on systemd >= 229.

rule.statement: Use `Type=simple` for Python/Node apps (uvicorn, celery, aiogram). Use `Type=oneshot` for scripts and migrations. Use `Type=forking` only for legacy daemons that fork by design.
rule.rationale: `Type=simple` means ExecStart IS the main process — systemd watches its PID directly. With `Type=forking`, systemd waits for the parent to exit and then tracks the child, which requires the app to support SIGTERM correctly. Most modern Python/Node apps do not fork.

rule.statement: Use `Restart=on-failure` with `RestartSec=5` for long-running services. Pair with `StartLimitBurst=5` and `StartLimitIntervalSec=60` in `[Unit]` to prevent infinite restart loops.
rule.rationale: `on-failure` restarts only on non-zero exit codes, signals, or timeout — it does not restart on clean shutdown (`systemctl stop`). The `StartLimitBurst=5` cap prevents a crashing service from consuming 100% CPU in a fast restart loop.

rule.statement: Set `MemoryMax`, `MemoryHigh`, `CPUQuota`, and `LimitNOFILE` on every production service. Without limits, a runaway worker can starve all other services on the VPS.
rule.rationale: `MemoryHigh` triggers memory reclaim before the hard cap; the service is throttled but not killed. `MemoryMax` is the hard backstop. `LimitNOFILE=65536` overrides the session-level PAM limit for this specific service, ensuring it is applied even if PAM is misconfigured.

example.code: [Service] MemoryMax=2G MemoryHigh=1500M MemorySwapMax=256M CPUQuota=200% LimitNOFILE=65536 TasksMax=256 OOMScoreAdjust=-300 OOMPolicy=stop

rule.statement: Load secrets via `EnvironmentFile=/home/nero/workspace/.env`. Never use `Environment=SECRET_KEY=xxx` in the unit file — it is visible in `systemctl show` output to any user who can query the service.
rule.rationale: `systemctl show nero-core` dumps all service properties, including inline `Environment=` values, in plaintext. `EnvironmentFile=` loads the file at start time but does not expose values via systemctl queries.

see-also.link: Linger, drop-in overrides, timer units, troubleshooting
